data.title: Wednesday's Economic Calendar
data.description:
7:00 AM MBA Mortgage Applications

The Mortgage Bankers' Association compiles various mortgage loan indexes. The purchase applications index measures applications at mortgage lenders.

8:30 AM PPI-Final Demand

The Producer Price Index (PPI) of the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) is a family of indexes that measures the average change over time in the prices received by domestic producers of goods and services.

PPI-FD is expected to be up the same 0.3 percent on the month and 2.7 percent on year in November after 0.3 percent and 2.7 percent in October.

8:30 AM Retail Sales

Retail sales measure the total receipts at stores that sell merchandise and related services to final consumers.

Modest increases are expected, with sales up 0.2 percent.

8:30 AM Current Account

The current account, on a quarterly basis, measures the U.S. international balance in goods and services trade as well as unilateral transfers.

9:50 AM Anna Paulson Speaks

Philadelphia Federal Reserve Bank President Anna Paulson speaks on the economic outlook before the Chamber of Commerce for Greater Philadelphia State of the Economy event.

10:00 AM Existing Home Sales 

Existing home sales tally the number of previously constructed homes, condominiums, and co-ops in which a sale closed during the month.

Forecasters see slightly better sales at an annual 4.23 million in December versus 4.13 million in November with lower mortgage rates.

10:00 AM Business Inventories

Business inventories are the dollar amount of inventories held by manufacturers, wholesalers, and retailers. 

Inventories are seen up 0.2 percent on the month.

10:00 AM Stephen Miran Speaks

Federal Reserve Board Governor Stephen Miran speaks on regulations, the supply side, and monetary policy before the Delphi Economic Forum Lecture event.

 10:30 AM EIA Petroleum Status Report 

The Energy Information Administration (EIA) provides weekly information on petroleum inventories in the U.S., whether produced here or abroad.

12:00 PM Raphael Bostic Speaks

Atlanta Federal Reserve Bank President Raphael Bostic participates in a moderated discussion on the economy as part of the Atlanta Business Chronicle 2026 Economic Outlook event.

12:00 PM Neel Kashkari Speaks

Minneapolis Federal Reserve Bank President Neel Kashkari participates in virtual town hall conversation hosted by the Wisconsin Bankers Association.

2:00 PM Beige Book

This book is produced roughly two weeks before the monetary policy meetings of the Federal Open Market Committee. 

2:10 PM John Williams Speaks

New York Federal Reserve Bank President John Williams gives opening remarks before "An Economy That Works for All: Financial Inclusion," an event organized by the Federal Reserve Bank of New York.

data.title: Trump dismisses Dimon’s criticism over DOJ probe of Fed chair Powell
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump pushed back against criticism from Jamie Dimon over the Justice Department’s probe into the Federal Reserve, saying the JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) chief was “wrong” to suggest the investigation undermines central bank independence.

“I think it’s fine what I’m doing,” Trump said. “And we have a bad Fed person.”

Dimon earlier raised concerns about the investigation into Jerome Powell, which centers on the cost of renovating the Fed’s headquarters and Powell’s subsequent congressional testimony.

“Everyone we know believes in Fed independence,” Dimon said. “And anything that chips away at that is probably not a great idea. And in my view, will have the reverse consequences. It’ll raise inflation expectations and probably increase rates over time.”

Responding to those remarks, Trump said: “I think he’s wrong.”

Trump said earlier Tuesday that he plans to move ahead with announcing a replacement for Jerome Powell within “the next few weeks,” despite backlash over the Justice Department probe.

Trump also defended his proposal to cap credit card interest rates at 10% for one year, a move that would likely require congressional approval. Banks have warned the cap could limit access to credit and weaken popular rewards programs.

“I think that people that are paying 28% interest should be protected,” Trump said. “We’re talking about for a one-year period.”

He added that he does not believe it is appropriate for “Jamie Dimon or anybody else” to charge customers high interest rates on credit cards.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: China touches record $1.2T trade surplus despite Trump tariffs
Spot silver surges past $90 for first time, gold nears all-time peak on Fed bets
data.description:
Spot silver (XAGUSD:CUR) surged past the key $90-an-ounce mark for the first time, driven by softer U.S. inflation data that reinforced expectations for Federal Reserve rate cuts, alongside geopolitical tensions, strong industrial and investment demand, and tightening supplies.

Silver prices jumped more than 1.5% to $90.94 an ounce at press time. Gold spot price (XAUUSD:CUR) also edged nearly 1% higher to $4,627.75 an ounce, close to an all-time peak.

Underlying U.S. inflation in December came in lower than expected, though economists cautioned the data may have been artificially subdued due to the record-long government shutdown late last year.

Precious metals have built on last year’s gains as the possibility of a criminal indictment against Jerome Powell reignited concerns over central bank independence. Global policymakers have voiced support for Powell, while Jamie Dimon of JPMorgan Chase & Co. warned that political interference could backfire.

Safe-haven demand has also been supported by U.S. President Donald Trump’s capture of Venezuela’s leader, renewed threats to seize Greenland, and violent protests in Iran that could destabilize the Islamic regime.

Against this backdrop, analysts at Citigroup raised their three-month forecasts for gold and silver to $5,000 an ounce and $100 an ounce, respectively.

ETFs: (GLD), (GDX), (GDXJ), (IAU), (NUGT), (PHYS),(GLDM), (AAAU), (SGOL), (RING), (BAR), (OUNZ), (SLV), (PSLV), (SIVR), (SIL), (SILJ)

data.title: Park Aerospace plans $50M manufacturing expansion to double capacity as demand surges
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Park Aerospace Corp. (PKE) Q3 2026

Management View

  • Brian Shore, Chairman & CEO, opened by referencing the company's third quarter earnings release and highlighted the filing of an S-3 registration statement with the SEC after the close. Shore stated, "Sales estimate was $16.5 million to $17.5 million, so we came in within that range. Adjusted EBITDA estimate was $3.7 million to $4.1 million. So we came in a little bit above that range." He clarified, "We don't do what's called guidance...we are telling you what we think will happen."
  • Shore discussed the ongoing impact of the ArianeGroup partnership, noting, "We had 0 sales of the fabric [C2B] in Q3, and we had a little bit more than $1 million of sales of the materials manufactured with C2B product in Q3." He explained the margin dynamics between fabric and manufactured materials sales, emphasizing the bottom-line impact of this mix.
  • The supply chain environment was described as improving yet challenged: "Industry challenges are reemerging as industry recovers and program ramps accelerate. This is actually a good thing, good news."
  • Mark A. Esquivel, President & COO, commented on tariffs: "We have minimal impact on tariffs in our Q3 just as we've had previously."
  • Shore highlighted major program wins, including the Marine Corps' selection of the Valkyrie for its collaborative combat aircraft program, and pointed to growing production on the A320neo program. He called attention to the company's exclusive involvement in the ramping Patriot missile programs, stating, "Park is sole source qualified for specialty ablative materials on the PAC-3 missile system program."
  • Shore announced plans for a major new composite materials manufacturing plant: "The new plant is being designed to be fully functioning and integrated...approximately double Park's current composite materials manufacturing capacity." The capital budget for the new plant is "approximately $50 million."

Outlook

  • Shore provided Q4 estimates: "Q4, $23.5 million to $24.5 million EBITDA of 4.75% of 5.25%." He clarified the expected Q4 margin impact: "Forecasted to include approximately $7.2 million of C2B fabric sales. So that's a small market, very, very light margins."
  • For the full year, Shore forecast, "$72.5 million to $73.5 million" in sales, with EBITDA reflecting the margin mix from C2B fabric sales.
  • Shore projected long-term composite materials sales outlook: "Our number is approximately $200 million," with the caveat that this is an outlook based on known programs and customers, not a formal forecast.

Financial Results

  • Shore reported Q3 sales of "$17.333 million; gross profit, $5,903,000; gross margin, 34.1%. Adjusted EBITDA, $4,228,000; adjusted EBITDA margin, 24.4%."
  • GE Aerospace program Q3 sales were "$7.5 million. Our forecast for Q4, $7.75 to $8.25 million and for the year, $29 million to $29.5 million."
  • The company ended Q3 with "$63.6 million of cash," and continues its record of "41 consecutive years of uninterrupted regular quarterly cash dividends."

Q&A

  • Operator: "It looks like we have no questions at this time. Therefore, I will turn it before back over to Mr. Brian Shore for closing remarks."
  • Shore thanked participants and offered to take follow-up questions via direct contact.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts did not present questions on the call, indicating a lack of immediate public concern or challenge.
  • Management maintained a confident and detailed tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing long-term opportunities and capacity expansion: "We are quite sure it is in Park's and our investors' very best interest for Park to be able to continue to exploit such opportunities as they arise in the future."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone remains optimistic and focused on execution, with management now providing more detailed numerical outlooks and explicit expansion plans.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q3 results showed sequential improvement in sales, gross margin, and adjusted EBITDA compared to Q2, with Q2 sales at $16.381 million and Q3 at $17.333 million, and gross margin increasing from 31.2% to 34.1%.
  • The Q4 sales forecast is significantly higher than Q3, but management cautioned about lower margin contributions from C2B fabric sales.
  • The new plant expansion budget was raised from $40–45 million in Q2 to $50 million in Q3, with a more concrete timeline and capacity impact described.
  • Previous quarter's Q&A focused on sales personnel and research coverage, while the current quarter had no analyst participation in the Q&A.
  • Management tone remains confident, with an increased focus on operational execution and capital deployment for growth.

Risks and Concerns

  • Shore identified supply chain challenges as a source of missed shipments: "Total missed shipments in Q3, approximately 740,000. That number is up quite a bit."
  • Tariff-related cost impacts remain minimal but are monitored for potential future changes.
  • Heavy reliance on key aerospace programs and defense contracts, with the timing and mix of fabric versus manufactured materials affecting margins.
  • The large capital investment in the new plant and the dependence on successful ramp-up of missile and aerospace programs present execution risks.

Final Takeaway

Park Aerospace Corp. reported strong Q3 results and outlined ambitious plans for a $50 million new composite materials plant, which is expected to double current manufacturing capacity and support surging demand from both aerospace and missile system programs. Management emphasized the company's position as a sole-source supplier for critical defense contracts, highlighted a robust cash position, and provided detailed sales and EBITDA projections for both Q4 and the full year. The call underscored Park's strategy to capitalize on long-term industry juggernauts by expanding capacity, maintaining operational flexibility, and leveraging key partnerships, while also acknowledging ongoing supply chain and margin mix challenges.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: U.S. tax season starts Jan. 26 as CPAs urge early organization
data.description:
The 2026 tax filing season in the United States will officially begin on Jan. 26, and taxpayers should start preparing now to avoid delays and errors, according to guidance from the Illinois CPA Society.

The Internal Revenue Service processed more than 163 million tax returns last year, and filing accurately and promptly can help reduce the risk of processing slowdowns or follow-up correspondence, the group said.

Build a checklist before filing

Before submitting federal and state returns, the Illinois CPA Society recommends creating a checklist of tax forms you expect to receive.

Because income sources vary widely, missing a single form is one of the most common reasons taxpayers later need to file amended returns.

Most tax documents arrive between January and February, though some may not be issued until March.

Common tax forms to watch for

Employers are required to send Form W-2 by the end of January, reporting wages, taxes withheld, and retirement contributions.

Homeowners may receive Form 1098 from mortgage lenders, detailing interest paid and certain insurance premiums.

Students and borrowers should watch for Forms 1098-T and 1098-E, which report tuition payments, scholarships, and student loan interest.

Investors typically receive several 1099 forms, including 1099-B for brokerage transactions, 1099-DIV for dividends, and 1099-INT for interest income.

Taxpayers who sell goods or services through platforms such as PayPal, Venmo, eBay, Etsy, or Cash App may receive Form 1099-K if transactions exceeded $20,000 or 200 payments in 2025.

Independent contractors earning more than $600 should expect Form 1099-NEC, while other miscellaneous income may be reported on Form 1099-MISC.

Those taking distributions from retirement accounts or pensions will receive Form 1099-R from their financial institutions.

Some forms arrive later

Business owners, partners, investors, and beneficiaries of trusts or estates often receive Schedule K-1 forms, which are commonly issued closer to mid-March or early April.

Taxpayers receiving Social Security benefits should also expect either Form SSA-1099 or SSA-1042S from the Social Security Administration.

Filing deadlines and reminders

The deadline to file individual tax returns is April 15, which is also the deadline to request a six-month extension.

An extension provides more time to file, but it does not extend the deadline to pay any taxes owed.

The IRS continues to encourage electronic filing and direct deposit for refunds to speed processing and improve security.

data.title: L3Harris missile spin highlights policy pressure and valuation questions, UBS says
data.description:
The decision by L3Harris Technologies (LHX) to accept a $1 billion government investment into a newly formed Missile Solutions Business reflects mounting pressure from Washington for defense contractors to accelerate production, according to BNP Paribas analyst Matthew Akers.

Under the plan, the Department of War will invest $1 billion in convertible preferred securities that will convert to equity upon a planned IPO of the missile unit in the second half of 2026. L3Harris would retain a controlling stake following the listing.

Akers in a Jan. 13 note to clients said the structure raises a central question now facing large defense primes: how much additional capital companies themselves are prepared to commit, as policymakers push for higher spending on capacity and research rather than shareholder returns.

Washington push reshapes capital priorities

The announcement follows a week of public pressure from the president and the Department of War urging contractors to move faster on production.

Akers noted that L3Harris (LHX) historically has spent a higher share of revenue on internal research and development than many peers, positioning it more favorably than others under the current policy backdrop.

Still, he said the government-backed investment underscores expectations that defense firms expand capital expenditure and R&D commitments beyond what markets have typically rewarded.

Missile assets seen as undervalued inside primes

Akers said the move also feeds into a broader investor debate about breaking up large defense contractors to unlock value.

He noted that Washington appears interested in rebuilding a defense “middle market” after decades of consolidation since the end of the Cold War.

Missile-focused assets, often embedded within diversified portfolios, are viewed as especially attractive given strong demand growth. Akers said investors have made similar arguments around missile exposure inside other large primes.

Higher multiple likely for missile pure-play

Defense companies currently trade around 16x forward enterprise value to earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization, according to BNP Paribas estimates. Akers believes a missile-focused company could command a valuation closer to the high end of the range, potentially near 20x.

On that basis, he estimates the Missile Solutions Business could reach an enterprise value of roughly $10 billion as a standalone public company.

However, Akers emphasized that the long-term outcome will depend on execution, capital allocation decisions, and how much incremental investment L3Harris (LHX) commits alongside the government funding.

data.title: Phoenix Education Partners reiterates $1.025B–$1.035B revenue outlook amid strong Q1 enrollment and AI-driven initiatives
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Phoenix Education Partners (PXED) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Christopher Lynne stated that the quarter "demonstrated disciplined execution of our strategy, marked by steady growth, strong retention and continued investment in student success and long-term value creation." He highlighted that first quarter revenue grew 2.9% year-over-year, with a 4.1% increase in average total degreed enrollment to 85,600 students, and noted that employer-affiliated enrollment now accounts for approximately 34% of total enrollment, up from about 31% in Q1 2025.
  • Lynne outlined the company’s approach to AI, emphasizing two priorities: preparing students for AI fluency through embedded content and leveraging AI institutionally to drive operational excellence. He reported, "We are encouraged with our progress, leveraging AI to improve outcomes across the student journey with examples that include the use of AI assistant appointment setting and outreach in certain situations to improve enrollment conversion and retention."
  • Addressing regulatory matters, Lynne said, "No new material areas of risk were introduced during the process, and we are pleased we will now have an accountability framework that applies equally to all programs at all institutions," referencing the consensus reached by the Negotiated Rulemaking Committee. He also noted that preliminary program performance accountability metrics showed all University of Phoenix programs for which metrics were provided are passing.
  • Regarding a cybersecurity incident, Lynne reported, "We recorded $4.5 million of expense associated with this incident," and assured that the university maintains comprehensive cybersecurity insurance and that the incident was addressed promptly.
  • Lynne announced the declaration of the inaugural regular quarterly cash dividend of approximately $0.21 per share, to be paid on February 18, 2026, and affirmed the company's commitment to disciplined capital allocation.
  • CFO Blair Westblom stated, "Net revenue increased 2.9% to $262 million, driven by a 4.1% increase in average total degreed enrollment to 85,600 students, supported by new student growth and retention gains from fiscal year 2025 continuing into first quarter 2026."
  • Westblom added, "Adjusted net income attributable to the company increased 5.3% to $53.6 million, up from $50.9 million in the prior-year period. Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter rose 7.2% to $75.2 million, and adjusted diluted earnings per share increased $0.03 to $1.38."

Outlook

  • Westblom reiterated the fiscal 2026 guidance: "Net revenue guidance of $1.025 billion to $1.035 billion and adjusted EBITDA guidance of $244 million to $249 million, both of which we provided on our November earnings call."
  • Management maintained its outlook from the previous quarter, emphasizing confidence in the full-year guidance and noting the solid start to the year.

Financial Results

  • Net revenue for Q1 was reported as $262 million, with an adjusted EBITDA of $75.2 million.
  • Adjusted EBITDA margin increased to 28.7%, up from 27.5% in the prior period. Cash and marketable securities totaled $218.1 million as of November 30, 2025.
  • General and administrative expenses rose to $106.6 million, primarily due to increased share-based compensation.
  • The company reported $4.5 million in expenses related to the cybersecurity incident and $29.5 million in noncash share-based compensation expense.
  • No outstanding debt was reported, and the company continues to maintain strong liquidity.

Q&A

  • Gregory Parrish, Morgan Stanley: Asked about regulatory impacts and fraud controls. Lynne responded that the accountability measures are "consistent with our early expectations," and reiterated, "We don’t anticipate... any material adverse impact from this regulation."
  • Alexander Paris, Barrington Research: Inquired about program coverage under new metrics and potential risk areas. Lynne explained that the preliminary data covered "a majority greater than 50% of our programs," and behavioral sciences may present some risk, but "we didn’t anticipate and continue not to anticipate this having any kind of material adverse impact on our programs."
  • Jasper Bibb, Truist Securities: Asked about second-half weighted enrollment growth. Lynne clarified that "revenue growth is going to be... unnaturally lower than our enrollment growth through Q3," due to last year's risk-free period student mix, but expects normalization in Q4.
  • Griffin Boss, B. Riley Securities: Probed enrollment growth by discipline. Lynne said growth was "broad-based across our programs." Boss also asked about ongoing cybersecurity and stock-based comp expenses. Lynne and Westblom indicated these would not be material ongoing costs.
  • Keen Fai Tong, Goldman Sachs: Sought details on debt-to-earnings test performance. Lynne stated, "Our average borrowing between trended in the right direction across all programs. Our average earnings trended in the right direction across all programs," and noted negligible risk from these metrics.
  • Stephanie Moore, Jefferies: Asked for updates on B2B employer engagement. Lynne highlighted growing employer alliances and new product offerings beyond degree programs.
  • Robert Sanderson, Loop Capital: Questioned pricing strategy and AI-related reskilling opportunities. Lynne indicated pricing has held constant since 2018 and highlighted a focus on affordability, while emphasizing AI integration into curriculum and institutional operations.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone, focusing on regulatory clarity, enrollment quality, and potential risks but acknowledging progress and improved controls.
  • Management's tone was confident, especially in prepared remarks, reinforced by statements such as "we feel pretty good about things there" and "we are encouraged with our progress." In Q&A, management maintained composure when addressing operational and regulatory questions, signaling continued confidence.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management sentiment remained steady, with confidence in operational execution and regulatory positioning, while analysts maintained a similar level of scrutiny.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company maintained its revenue and adjusted EBITDA guidance from the previous quarter, reiterating confidence in full-year targets.
  • Management continued to emphasize operational discipline, AI-driven enhancements, and student outcomes, consistent with prior strategic priorities.
  • Analysts’ focus shifted slightly from IPO-related execution and enrollment productivity in Q4 2025 to regulatory metrics, risk mitigation, and the sustainability of operational improvements in Q1 2026.
  • Key metrics such as average total degreed enrollment, adjusted EBITDA margin, and liquidity improved quarter-over-quarter.
  • Management’s tone remained confident and measured, while analysts sustained a neutral to slightly positive tone.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged potential ongoing expenses related to the recent cybersecurity incident, but does not expect them to be material due to insurance coverage.
  • Regulatory risks were addressed, with management stating that new rules are "consistent with our early expectations" and do not introduce material new risks.
  • Fraud and unusual enrollment activity remain industry-wide concerns, but management reported that controls are effective and no new material issues have arisen.

Final Takeaway

Phoenix Education Partners began fiscal 2026 with strong enrollment growth and operational progress, underpinned by disciplined execution and AI-driven initiatives. The company maintained its full-year revenue and adjusted EBITDA guidance, citing a solid financial foundation and effective risk mitigation. Management remains focused on delivering value through student outcomes, technology enhancements, and strategic capital allocation, reinforcing confidence in achieving long-term objectives.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Crude oil extends rally after Trump tells Iranian protesters 'help is on its way'
data.description:
Crude futures settled Tuesday at their highest level since late October after President Trump imposed tariffs on countries that do business with Iran and urged Iranians to continue protesting, saying on Truth Social that "help is on its way," prompting worries of rising tensions and potential disruption to global oil supply.

Iran, one of the top OPEC producers at ~3.3M bbl/day, is facing its biggest anti-government demonstrations in years, and a government crackdown against protesters has killed ~2,000 people and led to the arrest of thousands more, an Iranian official said.

Trump said he had canceled meetings with Iranian officials until protester deaths stopped, a day after stating that any country doing business with Iran will be subjected to a 25% tariffs on any business conducted with the U.S., which potentially could reignite a trade war with China, the biggest customer for Iranian crude.

"Betting markets are speculating on a U.S. military strike against Iran," Commerzbank's Carsten Fritsch said in a note. "This could also target the oil infrastructure in order to deprive the regime of its oil sales revenues."

However, if the Iran regime were to be overthrown, U.S. sanctions likely would be lifted and Iran's oil output could surge back to its full potential since the country is not constrained by OPEC+ production quotas, Fritsch added.

Barclays analysts said the unrest in Iran has added $3-$4/bbl in geopolitical risk premium to oil prices.

Four Greek-managed oil tankers were struck by unidentified drones in the Black Sea on the way to load oil at the Caspian Pipeline Consortium terminal off the Russian coast, Reuters reported.

Traders also continue to watch developments in Venezuela after Trump said last week that the government would hand over as much as 50M barrels of sanctioned oil to the U.S.

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for February delivery closed +2.8% to $61.15/bbl, and front-month Brent crude (CO1:COM) for March delivery finished +2.5% to $65.47/bbl, the fourth straight daily gain and highest settlement for both benchmarks since October 27.

U.S. natural gas futures (NG1:COM) maintained the previous session's strong gains, supported by colder weather due into the weekend and beyond; the front-month Nymex February natgas contract ended +0.3% to $3.419/MMBtu.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (XLE)

data.title: Concrete Pumping Holdings outlines $390M–$410M 2026 revenue target while advancing $22M fleet investment ahead of emission standards
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Concrete Pumping Holdings (BBCP) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Bruce Young stated that "our results continue to demonstrate the durability of our operating model and the benefit of our diversified platform despite a challenging macroeconomic backdrop." U.S. Concrete Pumping volumes remained stable in commercial markets, with infrastructure showing improvement but residential construction facing pressure. Young emphasized that "Eco-Pan Waste Management Services segment again delivered steady year-over-year growth, underscoring the benefits of our diversified platform."
  • Young announced an accelerated $22 million investment into the U.S. Concrete Pumping and Eco-Pan fleet for fiscal 2026, pulled forward from 2027, in anticipation of stricter NOx emission standards set to take effect January 1, 2027. This strategic move aims to mitigate expected disruptions and cost increases related to new emission regulations and to "improve the company's competitive positioning."
  • Young reported the completion of an acquisition in the Republic of Ireland in November 2025, describing it as "modest in size" but aligned with strategy, adding complementary capabilities in a region with "healthy long-term demand drivers."
  • CFO Iain Humphries reported, "Revenue was $108.8 million compared to $111.5 million in the prior year quarter ... driven primarily by the prolonged high interest rate environment." Humphries further explained, "Revenue in our U.S. Concrete Waste Management Services segment ... increased 8% to $21.3 million compared to $19.8 million in the prior year quarter."

Outlook

  • The company expects fiscal 2026 revenue to range between $390 million and $410 million, and adjusted EBITDA to range between $90 million and $100 million. Humphries stated, "Our guidance assumes no meaningful recovery in the construction markets during fiscal year 2026."
  • Free cash flow for 2026 is expected to be at least $40 million, with $23 million net replacement CapEx and $32 million net cash paid for interest, excluding the $22 million accelerated CapEx.
  • Humphries clarified, "This represents a timing shift rather than a structural change to our long-term capital framework."

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter revenue was $108.8 million. U.S. Concrete Pumping segment delivered $72.2 million, while Eco-Pan reached $21.3 million and U.K. operations reported $15.3 million.
  • Fourth quarter gross margin declined to 39.8% from 41.5% year-over-year. General and administrative expenses were $26.5 million, or 24.4% of revenue.
  • Net income available to common shareholders was $4.9 million or $0.09 per diluted share. Consolidated adjusted EBITDA was $30.7 million with a margin of 28.2%.
  • Total debt stood at $425 million, with net debt of $380.6 million and available liquidity of approximately $360 million.
  • The company repurchased approximately 274,000 shares for $1.8 million in the quarter, with $18.5 million remaining in the authorization.

Q&A

  • Timothy Mulrooney, William Blair: Asked about drivers behind expected revenue growth and whether the growth is from acquisition or organic. Humphries replied, "expecting volume to be largely consistent year-over-year, but we do expect to see some pricing improvement ... the volume to be relatively flat year-over-year."
  • Mulrooney: Inquired about margin pressure in 2026. Humphries responded, "It's mostly fleet utilization ... there's a marginal decline in that margin percentage ... from that lower-than-expected or optimal utilization."
  • Mulrooney: Questioned the outlook for residential construction. Young answered, "The regions where we do most of our residential, it was a little softer last year, but it's starting to improve slightly. And we do expect that it should improve some during this year."
  • Brent Thielman, D.A. Davidson: Sought high-level views on U.K. and Eco-Pan outlook. Young said, "We expect the public spend to be really good in our revenue in the U.K. to be quite strong ... Eco-Pan should be back to high single digits, maybe double-digit growth."
  • Thielman: Asked if Eco-Pan growth relies on new markets. Young replied, "There's not a lot of opportunity to create greater density in some of the current markets that we're already in."
  • Thielman: Queried whether the CapEx pull forward addresses all regulatory requirements. Young confirmed, "this pulling it forward will address almost all of that issue."
  • Andrew J. Wittmann, Baird: Asked about Eco-Pan margins given revenue growth. Humphries explained, "there is a little bit of overhead investment to stand up some of those new regions ... there's a bit of an investment lag."
  • Wittmann: Asked about fuel costs. Humphries stated, "year-over-year in the quarter, they were largely flat ... Our assumption is going forward, that will largely remain so we don't see it or like a headwind or a benefit going into next year."
  • Wittmann: Inquired about Ireland acquisition strategy. Young said, "We see opportunity for several other opportunities for acquisitions in Ireland."
  • Wittmann: Asked about project cancellations and backlog. Young noted, "office buildings that were planned over the last few years, they've been shelved ... manufacturing, there's a lot of that, that is on hold."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was neutral, with questions focusing on growth sources, margin pressures, and the outlook for individual business segments. Most inquiries sought clarification rather than expressing skepticism or concern.
  • Management’s tone was steady and pragmatic in both prepared remarks and Q&A, emphasizing operational discipline, strategic investments, and cautious optimism for select end markets. Young showed confidence, stating, "We are proactively accelerating a $22 million investment ... to improve the company's competitive positioning."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management maintained a similarly disciplined approach but highlighted more proactive capital allocation and explicit responses to regulatory challenges. Analyst tone remained consistent quarter-over-quarter, focused on operational details and strategic outlook.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The 2026 outlook projects higher revenue ($390 million–$410 million) compared to the previous year’s $380 million–$390 million guidance, but a reduction in EBITDA guidance ($90 million–$100 million) from the prior $95 million–$100 million range.
  • Management accelerated $22 million in fleet investment to 2026, a change from the previous capital allocation plan, directly addressing upcoming emission regulations.
  • The Ireland acquisition and commentary on further European expansion marked a notable strategic shift.
  • Margin concerns persist, with fleet utilization and volume flatness highlighted as drivers, echoing previous quarter discussions.
  • Analysts continued to probe margin drivers and segment outlooks, mirroring the prior quarter’s focus but with more attention to regulatory impacts and international growth.
  • Sentiment among management and analysts remained consistent, with confidence in operational resilience and cautious optimism for market recovery.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited continued pressure from high interest rates, leading to softness in residential and light commercial volumes.
  • Regulatory risk was identified with the 2027 NOx emission standards, prompting accelerated capital investment.
  • Young acknowledged uncertainty in commercial markets: "the downside is there's still no office buildings, manufacturing because of tariff concerns, really hasn't come about like we would expect it to."
  • Delayed or shelved commercial and manufacturing projects were highlighted as ongoing challenges.
  • Analysts raised questions about margin sustainability, segment resilience, and the strategic rationale for international expansion.

Final Takeaway

Concrete Pumping Holdings is proactively addressing both industry regulation and market uncertainty by accelerating a $22 million fleet investment in advance of stricter emission standards and targeting 2026 revenue between $390 million and $410 million. The company remains focused on maintaining operational flexibility, expanding selectively in international markets, and managing margin pressures through disciplined cost control and strategic capital deployment. Management maintains confidence in the durability of its platform and its readiness to capitalize on recovery as market conditions improve.
